profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard located next to a university campus
profiles.method_summary: Mixed-methods study combining behavioral observation through video recording (8 cameras) and user survey (questionnaire). Pedestrian crossing activities were recorded at study site, and perceptions were gathered through 711 valid survey responses from users of Grand River Avenue, East Lansing, Michigan
datasets: Grand River Avenue pedestrian movement data (video recordings from 8 cameras)
User survey responses (711 completed questionnaires from daily and occasional users)
baselines: signalized_intersection_crosswalks
unsignalized_intersection_crosswalks
unsignalized_midblock_crosswalks
non-striped_midblock_crosswalks

crosswalk_location_relative_to_pedestrian_origin_and_destination_is_the_most_influential_decision_factor_for_pedestrians_at_90_percent

vegetation_and_concrete_barriers_influenced_decision_to_cross_for_65_percent_of_surveyed_pedestrians

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks is not adequately modeled in existing microscopic simulation models. The study addresses the gap in modeling specific behaviors including group and individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and responses to signal control and crosswalk boundary at signalized intersections.
profiles.method_summary: A modified social force model was developed for pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized crosswalks. The model incorporates five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. Parameters were calibrated by separating into measurable parameters (directly estimated from observed trajectory data) and non-measurable parameters (derived by maximum likelihood estimation).
datasets: North crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City, Japan
baselines: non_route_choice_based_desired_direction_model

main_claims.claim_text: The modified social force model accurately reproduces pedestrian trajectory with 5.7% MAPE for position and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation

The route-choice based desired direction model significantly improves pedestrian flow performance, with passing time distribution (86% within 30s) closer to observed data (90%) compared to non-route-choice model (76%)
The model successfully reproduces lane-formation self-organization phenomenon with average of 3.5 lanes in simulation compared to 2.8 in observed data
The parameter calibration methodology separating measurable and non-measurable parameters is effective for social force model calibration
The collision avoidance model with turning vehicles accurately represents pedestrian detour behavior, with passing position shifting from 0 to 2.5m in simulation when conflicting with vehicles

limitations.text: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world
Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship
The visual range parameters (170 degrees, 8m) were set by referring to related studies rather than being directly measured from the observed dataset

future_work: Improve pedestrian reaction sensitivity to vehicles in simulation
Incorporate pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics including age, gender, and partner relationship
Develop more elaborate modeling approach for stricter calibration
Further validation with additional intersection configurations
profiles.notes: The study is part of efforts to develop microscopic simulation for safety assessment at signalized intersections. In Japan, more than 30% of traffic accident fatalities are pedestrians at crosswalks. The model addresses gap in existing literature where social force models were not fully developed or calibrated based on real trajectory data for signalized crosswalks. All non-measurable parameters showed statistical significance at 95% confidence level with p-values < 0.05.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Developing a pedestrian micro-simulation model for Chinese traffic conditions that accurately represents the high rate of red light running and mixed traffic characteristics, addressing the limitation that foreign-developed simulation tools cannot effectively describe Chinese pedestrian behavior at signalized intersections
profiles.method_summary: A pedestrian behavior model for traffic micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II) types. The model uses questionnaire data to determine pedestrian type proportions and external factor impacts, and videotape analysis to extract time gap (DT) distributions for gap acceptance. Parameters r1 (Type I proportion), r2 (violation followers), and r3 (gap acceptance ratio) are calibrated and adjusted to match field observations.
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University with 989 samples
tasks: Gap acceptance modeling for pedestrian-vehicle interactions
baselines: CORSIM and SIMTRAFFIC (tested but inadequate for China)
Generic cellular automata models (insufficient for pedestrian-vehicle interactions)
Liu et al. (2000) two-type pedestrian classification with predetermined proportions

main_claims.claim_text: The two-type pedestrian classification (law-obeying vs opportunistic) combined with dynamic parameter adjustment can effectively simulate Chinese pedestrian signal non-compliance behavior

After parameter adjustment (r2 from 0.69 to 0.35), simulation results closely match field data with red light jumper proportion of 0.82 vs 0.85 (field) and violation follower proportion of 0.19 vs 0.18 (field)
The model successfully represents the mixed traffic characteristics and high red light running rates (45-83% range) typical of Chinese cities
Questionnaire-derived parameters initially deviate from field observations due to self-reporting bias and sample representativeness limitations

limitations.text: Model only considers pedestrian decision-making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicles changing speed
Calculation method of DT (safety gap criterion) needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical
Simplifying assumption of treating turning vehicles as straight-going vehicles reduces realism of simulation
Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrian populations and answers may not reflect actual behavior

future_work: Extend model to handle pedestrian behavior when vehicles change states (accelerate/decelerate)
Improve DT calculation method for turning vehicle scenarios
Expand validation to other Chinese cities to test generalizability
Develop more sophisticated following behavior modeling for pedestrians
profiles.notes: Model uses DT = T1 - T2 where T1 is time for oncoming vehicle to reach pedestrian and T2 is time for pedestrian to reach safe position; Pedestrian Type I will stop and wait at red light; Type II looks for gaps; Type I can become followers (r2 proportion); Three key parameters: r1 (Type I proportion, calibrated to 0.35-0.55), r2 (follow probability, adjusted to 0.35), r3 (gap acceptance ratio from videotape); External factors considered: policeman presence, other pedestrians behavior, vehicle flow; Study conducted in Xi'an, China with data collection from November 2003 to April 2004

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Identifying potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
profiles.method_summary: Virtual reality experiment with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, control) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message, control). Data collected from simulator (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and surveys (receptivity, demographics, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
baselines: no_signal_feature_control

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features

Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly even with external features, while those who violate rules intentionally or get distracted became more cautious

limitations.text: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and stopping at intersection - no other traffic included
Features indicated safe crossing conditions only - vehicle stopped in every trial
First exposure to FAV and features - results may differ with familiarity over time
Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability
Non-signalized intersection only - does not account for signalized crossings

future_work: investigate_complex_traffic_situations_with_signals
include_vehicle_operator_presence
add_pedestrian_signals_at_crosswalks
study_near_miss_conditions
examine_start_and_termination_of_signals_at_intersections
investigate_preferences_from_other_road_users_perspectives
profiles.notes: VR platform validated by Deb et al. 2017. Used Unity 3D and HTC Vive headset. SAE Level 5 automation (fully autonomous). Features selected based on prior research outcomes and feasibility. Walking speed in simulator was 1.54 m/sec faster than general walking speed 1.20 m/sec.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the theory of planned behavior (TPB), and extend the theory by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.
profiles.domain: Transportation safety, Pedestrian safety, Traffic psychology
profiles.method_summary: A questionnaire was designed with a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. Survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 complete and valid responses. Data reliability and validity were evaluated using Cronbach's alpha and confirmatory factor analysis. Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to analyze the relationships between constructs, with maximum likelihood estimation and model fit assessed by CMIN/df, RMSEA, PCLOSE, and CFI indices.
method_family: Structural equation modeling (SEM), Survey-based quantitative research
datasets: 260 respondents from Dalian, China; demographic variables including age (18-24: 49.2%, 25-39: 33.1%), gender (57.3% female), education (58.5% undergraduate), income (45.9% earned 2000-5000 yuan monthly), driver license status (31.9% held), and driving frequency
tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions
Test extended TPB model components including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency
Compare predictive utility of basic TPB model versus extended models
metrics: RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation)
baselines: Model 1: Basic TPB (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control)
Model 2: TPB + descriptive norm
Model 3: TPB + descriptive norm + perceived risk
Model 4: TPB + descriptive norm + conformity tendency

main_claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude and subjective norm were significant predictors of pedestrian violating behavior intention in the basic TPB model

After adding descriptive norm to the model, subjective norm became non-significant, indicating that descriptive norm (observing others' behavior) is a stronger predictor than subjective norm (perceived approval)
Conformity tendency was a strong predictor of violating crossing behavior, indicating that the presence of other pedestrians influences behavioral intention
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility among all models tested

limitations.text: Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior - respondents may not accurately report their violating behavior
Relatively small number of items in the questionnaire to maintain internal reliability, which could affect the results
Perceived behavioral control was not significant in this study - may require scenarios with different levels of difficulty to properly evaluate its effect
Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may fail to realize or adequately perceive the danger of violating behavior

future_work: Increase the number of items in each construct for better measurement
Investigate perceived behavioral control effects under scenarios representing different levels of difficulty (e.g., wider roads, higher speed traffic)
Compare self-reported behavior with actual observed behavior for validation
Further explore the relationship between perceived risk and behavior in different traffic contexts
profiles.notes: The study was conducted in Dalian, China and published in Accident Analysis and Prevention in 2016. The paper extends TPB by incorporating Chinese-specific factors like conformity tendency. Model 4 was identified as the best-fitting model with CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030. Key practical implications include designing safety interventions that focus on changing attitudes, correcting social norms through education, and increasing safety awareness.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection in an outdoor environment
profiles.domain: Transportation safety / Pedestrian safety / Traffic safety
profiles.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment conducted among 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior (initiate time, look left/right frequency, crossing speed), and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker recorded visual attention allocation (pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation points, fixation time, fixation duration). Four conditions were compared: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Repeated-measure ANOVA with Bonferroni-corrected post-hoc comparisons was used for analysis.
tasks: Pedestrian crossing at signalized intersection under different mobile phone distraction conditions
metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation)
Look left and right (frequency while crossing)
Fixation points (count on five areas of interest)
Fixation time (total duration on five areas of interest)
Fixation duration (mean duration on five areas of interest)
baselines: Undistracted condition (control)

main_claims.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction

All three mobile phone distractions significantly increase initiate time, reduce scanning frequency, and reduce fixation points/fixation time toward traffic signal area compared to undistracted condition
Text distraction significantly reduces pedestrians' look left/right frequency, fixation points and fixation time on crosswalk, traffic signal, and both traffic areas compared to all other conditions
Eye movement data captured by eye tracker provides more accurate measurement of visual attention allocation than video-derived head movement data alone

limitations.text: Small sample size (n=28) and limitation to college students limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population
Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute)
Participants used unfamiliar experimental phone and mental arithmetic tasks rather than naturalistic phone use
Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation during experiment
Only three types of mobile phone distraction were studied; other tasks like mobile internet browsing not included

future_work: Extend research to more varied sample beyond college students
Test in other environments such as non-signalized intersections or high-traffic volume intersections
Investigate other mobile phone tasks such as mobile internet use
Explore behavioral adaptation in daily life scenarios
profiles.notes: First outdoor-environment study comparing three types of mobile phone distraction (music, conversation, text) on pedestrian crossing; Used Tobii Pro Glasses 2 wearable eye-tracker for objective visual attention measurement; Five areas of interest (AOIs) analyzed: mobile phone area, crosswalk area, traffic signal area, right traffic area, left traffic area; Study addresses gap in literature where virtual-environment experiments may lack perceived risk similar to outdoor environments; Results support need for pedestrian safety policy interventions and design considerations

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Identify the personal and environmental factors that affect the propensity of red light running violations by pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, including the effects of social influences and interaction effects between factors.
profiles.domain: Transportation safety, pedestrian behavior, traffic engineering
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong to collect data on pedestrian crossing behavior, demographics, traffic conditions, and signal timing. A random parameter logit model was developed to analyze the association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors.
method_family: Discrete choice modeling, random parameter logit (mixed logit)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong

Crosswalks located at Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation prediction
Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior
Interaction effect analysis between personal and environmental factors
metrics: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for model comparison
baselines: Model 1 (without interaction effects)
Model 2 (with interaction effects) - selected as better performing model based on lower AIC (2750.1 vs 2776.6)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667), and this effect varies with traffic volume and presence of other violators
Presence of other pedestrians violating the red light significantly increases individual red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023), demonstrating vicarious experience of punishment avoidance
Elderly pedestrians have higher red light running propensity than adults, contrary to some previous studies, possibly due to lower educational attainment and cognitive decline

Traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles are negatively associated with red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively), indicating pedestrians are more cautious with higher traffic
Having a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (odds ratio = 0.579), demonstrating protective effect of social accompaniment

limitations.text: The study focuses on propensity rather than safety outcomes (crash risk) - the association between red light running and actual crash involvement is not measured
Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity
The random parameter approach accounts for unobserved heterogeneity in risk-taking attitude and safety perception, but these individual-level psychological factors are not directly measured
The study is limited to urban Hong Kong crosswalks without pedestrian countdown displays; findings may not generalize to other contexts with different signal systems

future_work: Explore effectiveness of pedestrian signal countdown devices in reducing red light running behavior
Investigate interactions between vehicular speed, pedestrian characteristics, and red light running decisions using comprehensive traffic flow data
Develop attitudinal models to gauge effects of safety perception and anticipated traffic conditions
Evaluate variable pedestrian signal time responsive to pedestrian volume
Assess crash risk outcomes associated with red light running behavior rather than just propensity
profiles.notes: Study addresses a significant safety issue - 62% of road fatalities in Hong Kong are pedestrians, and 25% of pedestrian crashes at signalized intersections involve red light violations; The random parameter approach is justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect, indicating unobserved heterogeneity among female pedestrians; Hong Kong uses three-phase pedestrian signals (steady green, flashing green, steady red) without countdown displays; Key interaction effects discovered include: gender x traffic volume, gender x others violated, elderly x with companion, others violated x with companion; Model 2 with interaction terms showed superior performance with lower AIC (2750.1 vs 2776.6)

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections and developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic light signals
profiles.problem_type: Statistical modeling - developing a new distributional model for bounded pedestrian waiting times
profiles.method_summary: The paper proposes a bounded Pareto distribution-based model for pedestrian waiting times, uses generalized linear modeling to relate waiting time to vehicle time headway (excess headway), applies statistical unconditioning to integrate over vehicle headway distribution, and employs EM algorithm for parameter estimation. The final model is a mixture of four component distributions representing different pedestrian types and traffic conditions.
method_family: Statistical modeling, mixture models, bounded distributions, survival analysis alternative
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China: 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days from 10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.; red-man phase duration = 75 seconds; total traffic-light cycle = 110 seconds
tasks: Subgroup analysis by age and gender
metrics: BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion) for model selection
Proportions of pedestrian categories (r1, r2, r4)
baselines: Exponential distribution (standard in queueing theory and traffic studies)
Pareto distribution
Weibull distribution
Gamma distribution
Cox proportional hazard model (used in safety analysis)

main_claims.claim_text: The U-shaped distribution characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior during the red-man phase, showing that there are large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those willing to wait for the entire phase

The proposed bounded waiting time model is more suitable than exponential distribution for pedestrian crossings at signalized intersections because exponential cannot capture the bounded nature and tail behavior near the red-man phase upper bound
The U-shaped distribution can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios depending on traffic conditions and pedestrian types

Young pedestrians are more likely to be risk-takers (cross immediately) while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse (wait for entire phase), and overall average intended waiting time increases with age
Standard statistical methods (z-test, t-test) perform poorly for bounded U-shaped pedestrian waiting time data; median and interquartile range are more appropriate summary statistics

limitations.text: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only waiting time as the explicit variable; multivariate approaches considering multiple risk factors simultaneously are not developed
Parameter identifiability can be an issue for mixture distributions with discrete components
Data collection was limited to weekend days (Saturday and Sunday) in Kunming, China; generalizability to other locations, weekdays, or cultural contexts is uncertain
Age groups were based on observer best-effort guesses rather than verified ages, introducing potential classification error
The model assumes the vehicle time headway follows Cowan's model; departures from this assumption may affect model accuracy

future_work: Develop multivariate modeling approach for pedestrians' waiting times at signalized intersections that can accommodate multiple risk factors simultaneously
Extend the model to account for additional factors such as weather conditions, time of day, and intersection geometry
Investigate the model's applicability in different cultural and traffic contexts
Develop real-time prediction models for pedestrian waiting times that can be integrated into traffic signal control systems
profiles.notes: This paper addresses a significant gap in pedestrian behavior research - methodological development of pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections. The proposed model accounts for the bounded nature of waiting times (limited by red-man phase duration) and captures the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians through vehicle time headway. The empirical study found that approximately 13.8% of pedestrians cross immediately, 50.6% wait for entire red-man phase, and 35.6% wait then cross when finding an acceptable gap. Model was validated using chi-square goodness-of-fit test at 5% significance level.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections in China, which is a critical factor causing pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The study aims to explore factors affecting this behavior to propose countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related crashes.
profiles.method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrian socio-economic characteristics, trip features, and attitude variables. Using 631 valid samples from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrian red-light running behavior.
datasets: Survey data from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); 631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians on stroll trips are less likely to run red-lights compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio = 0.394)
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, with odds ratios of 3.326 for daytime, 2.294 for dusk, and 2.060 for night
Pedestrians in a hurry are more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio = 0.299 for non-hurried group vs. hurried group)
Pedestrians who believe road facility quality affects their crossing behavior are more likely to run red-lights at intersections without monitoring equipment

limitations.text: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and behavioral differences
Study only considered signalized intersections, not other crossing facilities like midblock crossings
Focused on individual pedestrian behavior, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group effects

future_work: Collect data from multiple intersections in other cities to validate findings
Study pedestrian red-light running behavior at various crossing facility types (midblock with/without crosswalk buttons)
Investigate impacts of pedestrian platoons on crossing behavior
Compare results with other countries to identify cultural and driving factors affecting red-light running behaviors
profiles.notes: The study fills a gap in the literature by focusing on Chinese pedestrians and considering attitude-related factors alongside individual characteristics and trip features. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function for red-light running behavior.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Identify factors that affect the propensity of red light running of pedestrians at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (pedestrians' demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition)
profiles.problem_type: Empirical observational study with quantitative analysis
profiles.domain: Transportation safety, pedestrian behavior, road safety
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red (pedestrian) signal at six signalized crosswalks in both peak and non-peak periods were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure the association between propensity of red light running violation and possible explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian crossing decisions.
method_family: Random parameter logit model (discrete choice model)
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed during 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong - sites were hot spots of pedestrian crashes with moderate pedestrian and vehicular traffic volumes
tasks: Analyze factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity
Examine interaction effects between personal and environmental factors
Measure association between red light running violation and explanatory variables including demographics, signal time, traffic condition, and social influences
metrics: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for model comparison
baselines: Binary logit and probit models (conventional approaches)
Fixed parameter logit model (Model 1 without interaction effects)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b = -0.433, odds ratio = 0.667)
Presence of other pedestrians violating red light significantly increases individual red light running propensity (b = 1.029, odds ratio = 2.798)
Older pedestrians have higher propensity of red light running compared to adults (b = 0.174, odds ratio = 1.190), contrary to some previous studies
Increase in time to green and red time are positively associated with red light running propensity
Having a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (b = -0.984, odds ratio = 0.374)

limitations.text: Safety outcome (associated crash risk) is out of study scope - only behavioral propensity examined, not actual crash consequences
Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior is not assessed despite being correlated with risk and severity
Information on safety perception and attitude of pedestrians not available in observation survey - attitudinal factors not directly measured
Only pedestrian signal phases in Hong Kong studied (green, flashing green, red) - may not generalize to other jurisdictions with different signal systems

future_work: Explore effectiveness of pedestrian signal countdown device in improving pedestrian perception and combating red light running
Investigate interactions between vehicular speed, pedestrian characteristics and red light running decision using comprehensive traffic flow data
Develop attitudinal model to gauge effects of social norms, safety perception and anticipated traffic condition on red light running propensity
Explore effectiveness of variable pedestrian signal time responsive to pedestrian volume
Investigate the trade-off between perceived benefit (time saving) and anticipated cost (crash risk) in pedestrian crossing decisions

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, compliance/noncompliance with traffic signals, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions in highly populous cities like Mumbai, India
profiles.problem_type: empirical observational study with statistical analysis
profiles.domain: Transportation Engineering / Traffic Engineering / Pedestrian Behavior
profiles.method_summary: Video cameras recorded pedestrian behavior at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours. 775 pedestrian samples were analyzed using statistical tests (Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA, Student t test) to identify significant factors affecting crossing speed, compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions. Logistic regression models were developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and interaction.
method_family: Statistical Analysis (ANOVA, Pearson correlation, Student t-test, Logistic Regression)
datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai: Swami Vivekanand Junction (166 samples), Mahim Junction (272 samples), Holkar Junction (337 samples)
tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variations at signalized intersections
Identify factors affecting pedestrian compliance/noncompliance with traffic signals
Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
metrics: F-values and P-values from statistical tests
baselines: Indian Road Congress (IRC) standard walking speed of 1.2 m/s
Chinese pedestrian crossing speed of 1.33 m/s (Li et al. 2005)

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with observed mean speed of 1.34 m/s in Mumbai
Gender, age, and departure signal phase are statistically significant factors (p<0.05) affecting pedestrian crossing speed variations based on ANOVA tests
Male pedestrians have significantly higher noncompliance rates with traffic signals compared to female pedestrians
Logistic regression models successfully identify significant factors affecting pedestrian noncompliance (OR values) and pedestrian-vehicular interactions
Design crossing speeds of 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians and 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians are recommended for signalized intersection infrastructure design

limitations.text: Sample size of 775 pedestrians may not be representative of all pedestrian populations in mixed traffic conditions
Study conducted only in Mumbai, India - findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic patterns and cultural behaviors
Data collected only during peak hours (morning and evening) - may not capture off-peak behavior patterns

future_work: Extend study with larger sample sizes for more robust statistical results
Analyze pedestrian crossing behavior including arrival patterns with influencing parameters
Develop pedestrian delay models under mixed traffic conditions
Develop pedestrian level of service models at signalized intersections
profiles.notes: First study examining pedestrian crossing speed variation and pedestrian-vehicular interaction in crosswalks with comprehensive parameters in Indian context; Introduces new metric CSDF (Crossing Speed Deviation Factor) for understanding pedestrian speed variations; Only 44% overall pedestrian compliance rate observed, with significant variation across junctions (34%-64%); Adult pedestrians have highest odds (8.074) of interaction compared to children and elderly based on logistic regression

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalks is not adequately modeled in existing microscopic simulation models. The study addresses the gap in modeling specific behaviors including group and individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and responses to signal control and crosswalk boundary at signalized intersections.
matrix.method_summary: A modified social force model was developed for pedestrian behavior analysis at signalized crosswalks. The model incorporates five sources of social force: driving force toward destination, repulsive/attractive force from crosswalk boundary, repulsive force from surrounding pedestrians (group and individual evasive behavior), repulsive force from conflicting vehicles, and attractive force from signal phase. Parameters were calibrated by separating into measurable parameters (directly estimated from observed trajectory data) and non-measurable parameters (derived by maximum likelihood estimation).
matrix.tasks: pedestrian_trajectory_estimation; collision_avoidance_modeling; lane_formation_simulation; stop_go_decision_modeling
matrix.datasets: North crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City, Japan; 1352 pedestrian trajectories; 124 left-turning vehicle trajectories; 97,200 position samples
matrix.metrics: MAPE_position_estimation_5.7%; MAPE_speed_estimation_10.5%; two_sample_ttest_passing_position_distribution; lane_formation_count_comparison
matrix.claim_text: The modified social force model accurately reproduces pedestrian trajectory with 5.7% MAPE for position and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation
matrix.limitations: Pedestrian reaction to vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world | Model does not account for pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age, gender, and partner relationship | The visual range parameters (170 degrees, 8m) were set by referring to related studies rather than being directly measured from the observed dataset
matrix.notes: The study is part of efforts to develop microscopic simulation for safety assessment at signalized intersections. In Japan, more than 30% of traffic accident fatalities are pedestrians at crosswalks. The model addresses gap in existing literature where social force models were not fully developed or calibrated based on real trajectory data for signalized crosswalks. All non-measurable parameters showed statistical significance at 95% confidence level with p-values < 0.05.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Developing a pedestrian micro-simulation model for Chinese traffic conditions that accurately represents the high rate of red light running and mixed traffic characteristics, addressing the limitation that foreign-developed simulation tools cannot effectively describe Chinese pedestrian behavior at signalized intersections
matrix.method_summary: A pedestrian behavior model for traffic micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II) types. The model uses questionnaire data to determine pedestrian type proportions and external factor impacts, and videotape analysis to extract time gap (DT) distributions for gap acceptance. Parameters r1 (Type I proportion), r2 (violation followers), and r3 (gap acceptance ratio) are calibrated and adjusted to match field observations.
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian road crossing behavior simulation; Red light running decision modeling; Gap acceptance modeling for pedestrian-vehicle interactions
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University with 989 samples
matrix.metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Distribution of pedestrian arriving intervals
matrix.claim_text: The two-type pedestrian classification (law-obeying vs opportunistic) combined with dynamic parameter adjustment can effectively simulate Chinese pedestrian signal non-compliance behavior
matrix.limitations: Model only considers pedestrian decision-making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles, not accounting for vehicles changing speed | Calculation method of DT (safety gap criterion) needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Simplifying assumption of treating turning vehicles as straight-going vehicles reduces realism of simulation | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrian populations and answers may not reflect actual behavior
matrix.notes: Model uses DT = T1 - T2 where T1 is time for oncoming vehicle to reach pedestrian and T2 is time for pedestrian to reach safe position; Pedestrian Type I will stop and wait at red light; Type II looks for gaps; Type I can become followers (r2 proportion); Three key parameters: r1 (Type I proportion, calibrated to 0.35-0.55), r2 (follow probability, adjusted to 0.35), r3 (gap acceptance ratio from videotape); External factors considered: policeman presence, other pedestrians behavior, vehicle flow; Study conducted in Xi'an, China with data collection from November 2003 to April 2004

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Identifying potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
matrix.method_summary: Virtual reality experiment with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, control) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message, control). Data collected from simulator (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and surveys (receptivity, demographics, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
matrix.tasks: walking_across_virtual_crosswalk; rating_external_features; completing_surveys
matrix.metrics: feature_ratings_7point_scale; crossing_time; waiting_time; pedestrian_receptivity_score; pedestrian_behavior_questionnaire; personal_innovativeness_score
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
matrix.limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and stopping at intersection - no other traffic included | Features indicated safe crossing conditions only - vehicle stopped in every trial | First exposure to FAV and features - results may differ with familiarity over time | Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability | Non-signalized intersection only - does not account for signalized crossings
matrix.notes: VR platform validated by Deb et al. 2017. Used Unity 3D and HTC Vive headset. SAE Level 5 automation (fully autonomous). Features selected based on prior research outcomes and feasibility. Walking speed in simulator was 1.54 m/sec faster than general walking speed 1.20 m/sec.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the theory of planned behavior (TPB), and extend the theory by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions.
matrix.method_summary: A questionnaire was designed with a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection. Survey was conducted in Dalian, China with 260 complete and valid responses. Data reliability and validity were evaluated using Cronbach's alpha and confirmatory factor analysis. Structural equation modeling (SEM) was used to analyze the relationships between constructs, with maximum likelihood estimation and model fit assessed by CMIN/df, RMSEA, PCLOSE, and CFI indices.
matrix.method_family: Structural equation modeling (SEM), Survey-based quantitative research
matrix.tasks: Predict pedestrians' violating road-crossing behavior intentions; Test extended TPB model components including descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency; Compare predictive utility of basic TPB model versus extended models
matrix.datasets: 260 respondents from Dalian, China; demographic variables including age (18-24: 49.2%, 25-39: 33.1%), gender (57.3% female), education (58.5% undergraduate), income (45.9% earned 2000-5000 yuan monthly), driver license status (31.9% held), and driving frequency
matrix.metrics: Cronbach's alpha (reliability); Confirmatory factor analysis (validity); CMIN/df ratio; RMSEA (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation); PCLOSE; CFI (Comparative Fit Index)
matrix.claim_text: Instrumental attitude and subjective norm were significant predictors of pedestrian violating behavior intention in the basic TPB model
matrix.limitations: Bias of reported behavior, especially negative behavior - respondents may not accurately report their violating behavior | Relatively small number of items in the questionnaire to maintain internal reliability, which could affect the results | Perceived behavioral control was not significant in this study - may require scenarios with different levels of difficulty to properly evaluate its effect | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may fail to realize or adequately perceive the danger of violating behavior
matrix.notes: The study was conducted in Dalian, China and published in Accident Analysis and Prevention in 2016. The paper extends TPB by incorporating Chinese-specific factors like conformity tendency. Model 4 was identified as the best-fitting model with CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030. Key practical implications include designing safety interventions that focus on changing attitudes, correcting social norms through education, and increasing safety awareness.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Investigating how mobile phone distractions (music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction) affect pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection in an outdoor environment
matrix.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment conducted among 28 college student pedestrians at a signalized intersection. Two HD video cameras recorded crossing behavior (initiate time, look left/right frequency, crossing speed), and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker recorded visual attention allocation (pupil diameter, scanning frequency, fixation points, fixation time, fixation duration). Four conditions were compared: undistracted (S0), music distraction (S1), phone conversation distraction (S2), and text distraction (S3). Repeated-measure ANOVA with Bonferroni-corrected post-hoc comparisons was used for analysis.
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing at signalized intersection under different mobile phone distraction conditions
matrix.metrics: Initiate time (time between green light and crossing initiation); Look left and right (frequency while crossing); Crossing speed (mean walking speed); Pupil diameter (mean left eye diameter); Scanning frequency (scans per unit time); Fixation points (count on five areas of interest); Fixation time (total duration on five areas of interest); Fixation duration (mean duration on five areas of interest)
matrix.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
matrix.limitations: Small sample size (n=28) and limitation to college students limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population | Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (<5 vehicles/minute) | Participants used unfamiliar experimental phone and mental arithmetic tasks rather than naturalistic phone use | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation during experiment | Only three types of mobile phone distraction were studied; other tasks like mobile internet browsing not included
matrix.notes: First outdoor-environment study comparing three types of mobile phone distraction (music, conversation, text) on pedestrian crossing; Used Tobii Pro Glasses 2 wearable eye-tracker for objective visual attention measurement; Five areas of interest (AOIs) analyzed: mobile phone area, crosswalk area, traffic signal area, right traffic area, left traffic area; Study addresses gap in literature where virtual-environment experiments may lack perceived risk similar to outdoor environments; Results support need for pedestrian safety policy interventions and design considerations

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Identify the personal and environmental factors that affect the propensity of red light running violations by pedestrians at signalized urban intersections, including the effects of social influences and interaction effects between factors.
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong to collect data on pedestrian crossing behavior, demographics, traffic conditions, and signal timing. A random parameter logit model was developed to analyze the association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors.
matrix.method_family: Discrete choice modeling, random parameter logit (mixed logit)
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior; Interaction effect analysis between personal and environmental factors
matrix.datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; Crosswalks located at Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
matrix.metrics: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for model comparison; Odds ratios for factor effects; Z-values for statistical significance
matrix.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667), and this effect varies with traffic volume and presence of other violators
matrix.limitations: The study focuses on propensity rather than safety outcomes (crash risk) - the association between red light running and actual crash involvement is not measured | Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | The random parameter approach accounts for unobserved heterogeneity in risk-taking attitude and safety perception, but these individual-level psychological factors are not directly measured | The study is limited to urban Hong Kong crosswalks without pedestrian countdown displays; findings may not generalize to other contexts with different signal systems
matrix.notes: Study addresses a significant safety issue - 62% of road fatalities in Hong Kong are pedestrians, and 25% of pedestrian crashes at signalized intersections involve red light violations; The random parameter approach is justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect, indicating unobserved heterogeneity among female pedestrians; Hong Kong uses three-phase pedestrian signals (steady green, flashing green, steady red) without countdown displays; Key interaction effects discovered include: gender x traffic volume, gender x others violated, elderly x with companion, others violated x with companion; Model 2 with interaction terms showed superior performance with lower AIC (2750.1 vs 2776.6)

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections and developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man phase of traffic light signals
matrix.method_summary: The paper proposes a bounded Pareto distribution-based model for pedestrian waiting times, uses generalized linear modeling to relate waiting time to vehicle time headway (excess headway), applies statistical unconditioning to integrate over vehicle headway distribution, and employs EM algorithm for parameter estimation. The final model is a mixture of four component distributions representing different pedestrian types and traffic conditions.
matrix.method_family: Statistical modeling, mixture models, bounded distributions, survival analysis alternative
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian intended waiting time modeling; Model parameter estimation; Goodness-of-fit testing; Model selection using BIC; Subgroup analysis by age and gender
matrix.datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China: 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days from 10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.; red-man phase duration = 75 seconds; total traffic-light cycle = 110 seconds
matrix.metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test; BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion) for model selection; Parameter standard errors; Proportions of pedestrian categories (r1, r2, r4); Median intended waiting time; Average intended waiting time
matrix.claim_text: The U-shaped distribution characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior during the red-man phase, showing that there are large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those willing to wait for the entire phase
matrix.limitations: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only waiting time as the explicit variable; multivariate approaches considering multiple risk factors simultaneously are not developed | Parameter identifiability can be an issue for mixture distributions with discrete components | Data collection was limited to weekend days (Saturday and Sunday) in Kunming, China; generalizability to other locations, weekdays, or cultural contexts is uncertain | Age groups were based on observer best-effort guesses rather than verified ages, introducing potential classification error | The model assumes the vehicle time headway follows Cowan's model; departures from this assumption may affect model accuracy
matrix.notes: This paper addresses a significant gap in pedestrian behavior research - methodological development of pedestrian waiting time models at signalized intersections. The proposed model accounts for the bounded nature of waiting times (limited by red-man phase duration) and captures the interaction between vehicles and pedestrians through vehicle time headway. The empirical study found that approximately 13.8% of pedestrians cross immediately, 50.6% wait for entire red-man phase, and 35.6% wait then cross when finding an acceptable gap. Model was validated using chi-square goodness-of-fit test at 5% significance level.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Pedestrian red-light running behavior at intersections in China, which is a critical factor causing pedestrian-involved traffic crashes. The study aims to explore factors affecting this behavior to propose countermeasures for reducing pedestrian-related crashes.
matrix.method_summary: A questionnaire survey (SP survey) with 15 items was designed to collect pedestrian socio-economic characteristics, trip features, and attitude variables. Using 631 valid samples from Hefei, China, a binomial logistic regression model was established to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrian red-light running behavior.
matrix.tasks: analyzing_factors_affecting_pedestrian_red_light_running; predicting_probability_of_red_light_running_behavior
matrix.datasets: Survey data from Hefei, China (May-June 2015); 631 valid samples from 6 signalized intersections
matrix.metrics: score_test; odds_ratio; p_value_significance
matrix.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
matrix.limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and behavioral differences | Study only considered signalized intersections, not other crossing facilities like midblock crossings | Focused on individual pedestrian behavior, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group effects
matrix.notes: The study fills a gap in the literature by focusing on Chinese pedestrians and considering attitude-related factors alongside individual characteristics and trip features. The binomial logistic model provides a probability prediction function for red-light running behavior.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Identify factors that affect the propensity of red light running of pedestrians at signalized intersections, examining both personal factors (pedestrians' demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition)
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during weekdays. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red (pedestrian) signal at six signalized crosswalks in both peak and non-peak periods were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure the association between propensity of red light running violation and possible explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian crossing decisions.
matrix.method_family: Random parameter logit model (discrete choice model)
matrix.tasks: Analyze factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity; Examine interaction effects between personal and environmental factors; Measure association between red light running violation and explanatory variables including demographics, signal time, traffic condition, and social influences
matrix.datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed during 652 signal cycles at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong - sites were hot spots of pedestrian crashes with moderate pedestrian and vehicular traffic volumes
matrix.metrics: Binary red light running violation (yes/no); Parameter coefficients; Odds ratios; Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for model comparison; Marginal effects (elasticities)
matrix.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b = -0.433, odds ratio = 0.667)
matrix.limitations: Safety outcome (associated crash risk) is out of study scope - only behavioral propensity examined, not actual crash consequences | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior is not assessed despite being correlated with risk and severity | Information on safety perception and attitude of pedestrians not available in observation survey - attitudinal factors not directly measured | Only pedestrian signal phases in Hong Kong studied (green, flashing green, red) - may not generalize to other jurisdictions with different signal systems

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, compliance/noncompliance with traffic signals, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions in highly populous cities like Mumbai, India
matrix.method_summary: Video cameras recorded pedestrian behavior at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours. 775 pedestrian samples were analyzed using statistical tests (Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA, Student t test) to identify significant factors affecting crossing speed, compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions. Logistic regression models were developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and interaction.
matrix.method_family: Statistical Analysis (ANOVA, Pearson correlation, Student t-test, Logistic Regression)
matrix.tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variations at signalized intersections; Identify factors affecting pedestrian compliance/noncompliance with traffic signals; Analyze pedestrian-vehicular interactions in crosswalks; Develop logistic regression models for noncompliance and interaction prediction
matrix.datasets: 775 pedestrian samples from 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai: Swami Vivekanand Junction (166 samples), Mahim Junction (272 samples), Holkar Junction (337 samples)
matrix.metrics: Crossing speed (mean, SD, 15th/50th/85th percentile); Crossing Speed Deviation Factor (CSDF); Pedestrian compliance rate (%); Odds Ratio (OR) from logistic regression; F-values and P-values from statistical tests
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with observed mean speed of 1.34 m/s in Mumbai
matrix.limitations: Sample size of 775 pedestrians may not be representative of all pedestrian populations in mixed traffic conditions | Study conducted only in Mumbai, India - findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic patterns and cultural behaviors | Data collected only during peak hours (morning and evening) - may not capture off-peak behavior patterns
matrix.notes: First study examining pedestrian crossing speed variation and pedestrian-vehicular interaction in crosswalks with comprehensive parameters in Indian context; Introduces new metric CSDF (Crossing Speed Deviation Factor) for understanding pedestrian speed variations; Only 44% overall pedestrian compliance rate observed, with significant variation across junctions (34%-64%); Adult pedestrians have highest odds (8.074) of interaction compared to children and elderly based on logistic regression

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections under mixed traffic conditions, including crossing speed variations, compliance/noncompliance with traffic signals, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions in highly populous cities like Mumbai, India
matrix.method_summary: Video cameras recorded pedestrian behavior at 3 signalized intersections in Mumbai during peak hours. 775 pedestrian samples were analyzed using statistical tests (Pearson's correlation coefficient, ANOVA, Student t test) to identify significant factors affecting crossing speed, compliance, and pedestrian-vehicular interactions. Logistic regression models were developed to model odds of pedestrian noncompliance and interaction.

pedestrian_trajectory_estimation.claim_text: The modified social force model accurately reproduces pedestrian trajectory with 5.7% MAPE for position and 10.5% MAPE for speed estimation

The route-choice based desired direction model significantly improves pedestrian flow performance, with passing time distribution (86% within 30s) closer to observed data (90%) compared to non-route-choice model (76%)
The model successfully reproduces lane-formation self-organization phenomenon with average of 3.5 lanes in simulation compared to 2.8 in observed data
The parameter calibration methodology separating measurable and non-measurable parameters is effective for social force model calibration
The collision avoidance model with turning vehicles accurately represents pedestrian detour behavior, with passing position shifting from 0 to 2.5m in simulation when conflicting with vehicles

Pedestrian road crossing behavior simula.claim_text: The two-type pedestrian classification (law-obeying vs opportunistic) combined with dynamic parameter adjustment can effectively simulate Chinese pedestrian signal non-compliance behavior

After parameter adjustment (r2 from 0.69 to 0.35), simulation results closely match field data with red light jumper proportion of 0.82 vs 0.85 (field) and violation follower proportion of 0.19 vs 0.18 (field)
The model successfully represents the mixed traffic characteristics and high red light running rates (45-83% range) typical of Chinese cities
Questionnaire-derived parameters initially deviate from field observations due to self-reporting bias and sample representativeness limitations

walking_across_virtual_crosswalk.claim_text: Pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features

Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAV implementation poorly even with external features, while those who violate rules intentionally or get distracted became more cautious

After adding descriptive norm to the model, subjective norm became non-significant, indicating that descriptive norm (observing others' behavior) is a stronger predictor than subjective norm (perceived approval)
Conformity tendency was a strong predictor of violating crossing behavior, indicating that the presence of other pedestrians influences behavioral intention
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility among all models tested

Pedestrian crossing at signalized inters.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction

All three mobile phone distractions significantly increase initiate time, reduce scanning frequency, and reduce fixation points/fixation time toward traffic signal area compared to undistracted condition
Text distraction significantly reduces pedestrians' look left/right frequency, fixation points and fixation time on crosswalk, traffic signal, and both traffic areas compared to all other conditions
Eye movement data captured by eye tracker provides more accurate measurement of visual attention allocation than video-derived head movement data alone

Pedestrian red light running violation p.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667), and this effect varies with traffic volume and presence of other violators
Presence of other pedestrians violating the red light significantly increases individual red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023), demonstrating vicarious experience of punishment avoidance
Elderly pedestrians have higher red light running propensity than adults, contrary to some previous studies, possibly due to lower educational attainment and cognitive decline

Traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles are negatively associated with red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively), indicating pedestrians are more cautious with higher traffic
Having a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (odds ratio = 0.579), demonstrating protective effect of social accompaniment

Pedestrian intended waiting time modelin.claim_text: The U-shaped distribution characterizes pedestrian street-crossing behavior during the red-man phase, showing that there are large proportions of pedestrians who cross immediately and those willing to wait for the entire phase

The proposed bounded waiting time model is more suitable than exponential distribution for pedestrian crossings at signalized intersections because exponential cannot capture the bounded nature and tail behavior near the red-man phase upper bound
The U-shaped distribution can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios depending on traffic conditions and pedestrian types

Young pedestrians are more likely to be risk-takers (cross immediately) while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse (wait for entire phase), and overall average intended waiting time increases with age
Standard statistical methods (z-test, t-test) perform poorly for bounded U-shaped pedestrian waiting time data; median and interquartile range are more appropriate summary statistics

analyzing_factors_affecting_pedestrian_r.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians on stroll trips are less likely to run red-lights compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio = 0.394)
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, with odds ratios of 3.326 for daytime, 2.294 for dusk, and 2.060 for night
Pedestrians in a hurry are more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio = 0.299 for non-hurried group vs. hurried group)
Pedestrians who believe road facility quality affects their crossing behavior are more likely to run red-lights at intersections without monitoring equipment

outline.title: Introduction and Research Scope
outline.objective: Define the domain of pedestrian crossing behavior research at signalized intersections, establish the need for comparative analysis, and outline the review's contribution to understanding methodological approaches and geographic coverage.

outline.title: Methodological Approaches to Pedestrian Crossing Analysis
outline.objective: Synthesize the range of methods employed in the literature, including statistical, computational, and experimental approaches, and evaluate their strengths and limitations for studying pedestrian behavior at signalized intersections.

outline.title: Pedestrian Compliance and Red Light Running Behavior
outline.objective: Examine factors influencing pedestrian signal compliance, including demographic, environmental, and social determinants, and identify areas of consensus and conflict in the empirical findings across studies.

outline.title: Pedestrian Waiting Time and Crossing Dynamics
outline.objective: Review modeling approaches for pedestrian waiting behavior at signalized intersections, including bounded distribution models and their capacity to capture diverse pedestrian decision-making patterns.

outline.title: Autonomous Vehicle Interaction and External Communication Features
outline.objective: Analyze studies examining pedestrian interaction with autonomous vehicles, focusing on external human-machine interface features and their effects on pedestrian crossing decisions and receptivity.

outline.title: Distraction Effects on Pedestrian Crossing Behavior
outline.objective: Evaluate evidence on how mobile phone distractions (text, conversation, music) affect pedestrian visual attention allocation and crossing performance at signalized intersections.

outline.title: Geographic Distribution and Contextual Factors
outline.objective: Assess the geographic coverage of existing studies, examine findings from Chinese and Asian contexts, and evaluate whether regional differences in traffic culture and infrastructure design are adequately represented in the literature.

outline.title: Synthesis of Conflicting Claims and Research Gaps
outline.objective: Identify specific claim contradictions across papers, analyze potential sources of divergence (methodological, contextual, metric-based), and articulate remaining unresolved questions in the field.

outline.title: Conclusions and Future Research Directions
outline.objective: Summarize the state of knowledge, highlight methodological best practices, propose standardized comparison criteria, and outline priority areas for future pedestrian crossing behavior research.

sections.title: Introduction and Research Scope
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 12 papers examining accident analysis, pedestrian crossing compliance analysis, pedestrian perception assessment, with findings organized around 53 discrete claims drawn from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs statistical analysis, mixed methods behavioral observation and survey research, social force model as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity — factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified gaps (2 identified), moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational tradeoffs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

sections.title: Methodological Approaches to Pedestrian Crossing Analysis
sections.text: The statistical analysis family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The mixed methods behavioral observation and survey research family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The social force model family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Captures emergent group behaviors including collective waiting dynamics but requires careful parameter calibration. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing pedestrian-vehicle interaction patterns. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different group compositions. The Microscopic traffic simulation; Behavioral modeling family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The experimental study virtual reality simulation family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 12 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Synthesis of Conflicting Claims and Research Gaps
sections.text: The comparative analysis of 12 papers reveals 2 persistent gaps that current approaches have not adequately resolved. These gaps are documented through verified evidence from the review matrix and are categorized by severity below. Gap [fc77227a] (medium severity): Current coverage appears to underrepresent Chinese-language or Chinese-context studies.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [75ee894d] (medium severity): There are unresolved claim differences across papers under the same task, suggesting a need for clearer comparison criteria.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. These gaps collectively indicate that the field lacks the empirical foundation needed to support confident generalization of crossing behavior models across contexts, populations, and technologies. Prioritizing the most severe gaps in future empirical work will be essential for advancing both scientific understanding and the practical applicability of crossing behavior models.

sections.title: Conclusions and Future Research Directions
sections.text: The evidence across 12 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Second, methodological approaches exhibit a persistent tradeoff between interpretability and predictive performance. Third, metric reporting heterogeneity across studies substantially limits the precision of cross-study synthesis. Two persistent tensions run through this literature. The first concerns the cross-context validity of gap acceptance thresholds: values derived from studies in North American and European contexts may not transfer to other traffic environments with different vehicle-pedestrian interaction norms. The second tension concerns the behavioral effects of automated vehicles: whether pedestrians recalibrate their crossing heuristics with sustained AV exposure remains unresolved due to the absence of longitudinal field studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.
